main_paper.raw: {"methods":["CALM (Competence-based Analysis of Language Models): a general interpretability framework for studying LLM competence using causal probing","Causal probing interventions to study LLMs' internal representations of linguistic properties","A new gradient-based adversarial attack methodology for causal probing, targeting structural probes and arbitrarily-encoded relational properties"],"problems":["Understanding the representations of linguistic structure learned by LLMs during pretraining","Explaining and predicting unexpected LLM behaviors in response to prompt variation or distribution shift","Measuring and comparing LLM competence in specific linguistic tasks","Distinguishing between models' use of causal and spurious properties in task performance"],"datasets":["14 lexical inference tasks (specific datasets not named in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Quantitative measure of LLM competence (alignment between ground-truth causal structure and LLM's latent representation)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of CALM, a general interpretability framework for LLM competence using causal probing","First quantitative measure of LLM competence based on causal alignment","Development of a new gradient-based intervention strategy for causal probing, extending to arbitrarily-encoded relational properties","Demonstration that CALM can explain and predict LLM behaviors across lexical inference tasks by distinguishing causal from spurious properties"]}

methods: CALM (Competence-based Analysis of Language Models): a general interpretability framework for studying LLM competence using causal probing
Causal probing interventions to study LLMs' internal representations of linguistic properties
A new gradient-based adversarial attack methodology for causal probing, targeting structural probes and arbitrarily-encoded relational properties
problems: Understanding the representations of linguistic structure learned by LLMs during pretraining
Explaining and predicting unexpected LLM behaviors in response to prompt variation or distribution shift
Measuring and comparing LLM competence in specific linguistic tasks
Distinguishing between models' use of causal and spurious properties in task performance
datasets: 14 lexical inference tasks (specific datasets not named in the provided content)
metrics: Quantitative measure of LLM competence (alignment between ground-truth causal structure and LLM's latent representation)
novelty_claims: Introduction of CALM, a general interpretability framework for LLM competence using causal probing
First quantitative measure of LLM competence based on causal alignment
Development of a new gradient-based intervention strategy for causal probing, extending to arbitrarily-encoded relational properties
Demonstration that CALM can explain and predict LLM behaviors across lexical inference tasks by distinguishing causal from spurious properties

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["CALM (Competence-based Analysis of Language Models) framework","Gradient-Based Interventions (GBIs) for causal probing of LLM representations","Targeted causal interventions on LLM internal representations"],"problems":["Brittleness of large language models to small input or context changes","Lack of understanding of the internal representations and competence of LLMs","Limitations of black-box behavioral analysis for diagnosing LLM weaknesses","Need for robust and interpretable language models"],"datasets":["14 lexical inference tasks (specific datasets not named in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Competence (alignment between ground-truth causal structure and LLM latent representation)","Generalization under distribution shift","Task performance on relation prompting tasks"],"results":[{"metric":"Competence (as measured by CALM)","value":"CALM can explain important patterns in LLM behavior across 14 lexical inference tasks by distinguishing use of causal vs. spurious properties"},{"metric":"Interpretability of representations","value":"Representations leveraged by LLMs are highly entangled but can be meaningfully interpreted in terms of task utilization"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of the CALM framework for competence-based analysis of language models using causal probing.","Development of Gradient-Based Interventions (GBIs), the first causal probing technique to target arbitrarily-encoded feature representations in LLMs.","Demonstration that CALM enables expanded and more precise analysis of LLM competence and weaknesses compared to prior methods."]}

methods: CALM (Competence-based Analysis of Language Models) framework
Gradient-Based Interventions (GBIs) for causal probing of LLM representations
Targeted causal interventions on LLM internal representations
problems: Brittleness of large language models to small input or context changes
Lack of understanding of the internal representations and competence of LLMs
Limitations of black-box behavioral analysis for diagnosing LLM weaknesses
Need for robust and interpretable language models
datasets: 14 lexical inference tasks (specific datasets not named in the provided content)
metrics: Competence (alignment between ground-truth causal structure and LLM latent representation)
Generalization under distribution shift
Task performance on relation prompting tasks

results.value: CALM can explain important patterns in LLM behavior across 14 lexical inference tasks by distinguishing use of causal vs. spurious properties
Representations leveraged by LLMs are highly entangled but can be meaningfully interpreted in terms of task utilization

novelty_claims: Introduction of the CALM framework for competence-based analysis of language models using causal probing.
Development of Gradient-Based Interventions (GBIs), the first causal probing technique to target arbitrarily-encoded feature representations in LLMs.
Demonstration that CALM enables expanded and more precise analysis of LLM competence and weaknesses compared to prior methods.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Classifier-based probing of language model internal representations using linear classifiers (probes) to assess linguistic phenomena","Comprehensive benchmark (Holmes) covering 208 datasets for probing linguistic competence","Control tasks and information-theoretic verification of probe results","Streamlined evaluation method (FlashHolmes) that reduces computation by selecting a subset of samples"],"problems":["Lack of comprehensive and disentangled evaluation of language models' linguistic competence (as opposed to linguistic performance)","Over-reliance on prompting-based benchmarks that conflate linguistic competence with other cognitive abilities","Underrepresentation of certain linguistic phenomena and large LMs in previous probing studies","High computational cost of evaluating LMs on large-scale probing benchmarks"],"datasets":["208 probing datasets covering morphology, syntax, semantics, reasoning, and discourse phenomena (as part of Holmes benchmark)"],"metrics":["Probe accuracy (performance of linear classifiers on probing tasks)","Correlation of model rankings with other benchmarks","Computational efficiency (percentage of computation required by FlashHolmes vs. full Holmes)"],"results":[{"metric":"Number of LMs evaluated","value":"59"},{"metric":"Number of datasets/tasks","value":"208"},{"metric":"Computation required by FlashHolmes","value":"~3% of full Holmes"},{"metric":"Correlation of Holmes rankings with reasoning-intense downstream tasks","value":"High (aligns with other benchmarks)"},{"metric":"Linguistic competence scaling","value":"Pronounced for morphology and syntax beyond 350M parameters"},{"metric":"Decoder vs. encoder LM stability","value":"Decoder-only LMs (even 70B) less stable than encoder-only (110M) for word representations"}],"novelty_claims":["Holmes is the first comprehensive benchmark to assess LMs' linguistic competence across a wide range of phenomena, disentangled from linguistic performance.","Holmes covers 208 datasets and 66 linguistic phenomena, including underrepresented areas like negation and rhetoric.","First systematic analysis of the effect of instruction tuning on linguistic competence, showing it improves morphology and syntax but has mixed effects elsewhere.","FlashHolmes provides a highly efficient approximation of full benchmark rankings, requiring only ~3% of the computation.","Holmes enables holistic and rigorous evaluation of LMs' linguistic competence, addressing gaps in previous probing studies."]}

methods: Classifier-based probing of language model internal representations using linear classifiers (probes) to assess linguistic phenomena
Comprehensive benchmark (Holmes) covering 208 datasets for probing linguistic competence
Control tasks and information-theoretic verification of probe results
Streamlined evaluation method (FlashHolmes) that reduces computation by selecting a subset of samples
problems: Lack of comprehensive and disentangled evaluation of language models' linguistic competence (as opposed to linguistic performance)
Over-reliance on prompting-based benchmarks that conflate linguistic competence with other cognitive abilities
Underrepresentation of certain linguistic phenomena and large LMs in previous probing studies
High computational cost of evaluating LMs on large-scale probing benchmarks
datasets: 208 probing datasets covering morphology, syntax, semantics, reasoning, and discourse phenomena (as part of Holmes benchmark)
metrics: Probe accuracy (performance of linear classifiers on probing tasks)
Correlation of model rankings with other benchmarks
Computational efficiency (percentage of computation required by FlashHolmes vs. full Holmes)

results.value: ~3% of full Holmes

results.metric: Correlation of Holmes rankings with reasoning-intense downstream tasks
results.value: High (aligns with other benchmarks)
Pronounced for morphology and syntax beyond 350M parameters
Decoder-only LMs (even 70B) less stable than encoder-only (110M) for word representations

novelty_claims: Holmes is the first comprehensive benchmark to assess LMs' linguistic competence across a wide range of phenomena, disentangled from linguistic performance.
Holmes covers 208 datasets and 66 linguistic phenomena, including underrepresented areas like negation and rhetoric.
First systematic analysis of the effect of instruction tuning on linguistic competence, showing it improves morphology and syntax but has mixed effects elsewhere.
FlashHolmes provides a highly efficient approximation of full benchmark rankings, requiring only ~3% of the computation.
Holmes enables holistic and rigorous evaluation of LMs' linguistic competence, addressing gaps in previous probing studies.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Causal abstraction analysis (three-stage methodology): (1) Formulate a high-level causal model hypothesis, (2) Align neural representations with variables in the causal model, (3) Experimentally verify causal properties using interchange interventions","Interchange intervention method for experimental verification of causal properties","Comparison with probing and integrated gradients (attribution) methods"],"problems":["Lack of systematic, formal methods for causal analysis and interpretability of neural network internal representations","Difficulty in providing high-level causal explanations for neural network behavior, especially in complex tasks like Multiply Quantified Natural Language Inference (MQNLI)"],"datasets":["Multiply Quantified Natural Language Inference (MQNLI) corpus"],"metrics":["Accuracy on MQNLI dataset","Causal alignment between neural representations and high-level variables (qualitative/structural)"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy on MQNLI (BERT-based model)","value":"88%"},{"metric":"Accuracy on MQNLI (LSTM-based model)","value":"46%"},{"metric":"Causal structure realization (BERT-based model)","value":"Partial realization of natural logic causal structure"},{"metric":"Causal structure realization (LSTM-based model)","value":"No realization of natural logic causal structure"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new structural analysis method grounded in formal causal abstraction theory for neural networks","Introduces a systematic methodology for aligning neural representations with interpretable causal models and verifying causal properties via interventions","Demonstrates, for the first time, that high-level causal explanations for neural network behavior can be achieved in complex NLI tasks","Shows that BERT-based models can encode compositional causal structure, while simpler models (LSTM) cannot, using the proposed methodology","Provides a framework for systematically measuring and aggregating causal properties to evaluate hypotheses about abstract causal structure"]}

methods: Causal abstraction analysis (three-stage methodology): (1) Formulate a high-level causal model hypothesis, (2) Align neural representations with variables in the causal model, (3) Experimentally verify causal properties using interchange interventions
Interchange intervention method for experimental verification of causal properties
Comparison with probing and integrated gradients (attribution) methods
problems: Lack of systematic, formal methods for causal analysis and interpretability of neural network internal representations
Difficulty in providing high-level causal explanations for neural network behavior, especially in complex tasks like Multiply Quantified Natural Language Inference (MQNLI)
datasets: Multiply Quantified Natural Language Inference (MQNLI) corpus
metrics: Accuracy on MQNLI dataset
Causal alignment between neural representations and high-level variables (qualitative/structural)

results.value: Partial realization of natural logic causal structure
No realization of natural logic causal structure

novelty_claims: Proposes a new structural analysis method grounded in formal causal abstraction theory for neural networks
Introduces a systematic methodology for aligning neural representations with interpretable causal models and verifying causal properties via interventions
Demonstrates, for the first time, that high-level causal explanations for neural network behavior can be achieved in complex NLI tasks
Shows that BERT-based models can encode compositional causal structure, while simpler models (LSTM) cannot, using the proposed methodology
Provides a framework for systematically measuring and aggregating causal properties to evaluate hypotheses about abstract causal structure

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Naturalistic causal probing (input-level intervention on naturalistic sentences to create counterfactuals)","Estimation of average treatment effect (ATE) of morpho-syntactic features on contextualized representations","Causal analysis of gender bias using naturalistic counterfactuals","Comparison of correlational probes with causal probes using augmented datasets"],"problems":["Limitations of traditional probing methods in identifying causal relationships in neural language model representations","Bias and lack of diversity in template-based counterfactual interventions","Overestimation of linguistic property encoding by correlational probes","Understanding how morpho-syntactic features (gender, number) are encoded and causally affect model representations"],"datasets":["Spanish corpora (with naturalistic sentences and their counterfactuals)"],"metrics":["Average Treatment Effect (ATE)","Probe accuracy (for gender and number prediction)"],"results":[{"metric":"Probe accuracy (correlational, unaltered data)","value":">90%"},{"metric":"Probe accuracy (correlational, augmented with naturalistic counterfactuals)","value":"~60% (close to chance)"},{"metric":"ATE stability","value":"Stable across corpora"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a naturalistic input-level intervention strategy to generate counterfactuals for causal probing, improving over template-based and representation-level interventions.","Demonstrates that naturalistic counterfactuals provide unbiased estimates of causal effects of morpho-syntactic features.","Shows that correlational probes overestimate the presence of linguistic properties, and that causal probing with naturalistic counterfactuals gives more accurate estimates.","Applies the method to analyze gender and number encoding and gender bias in pre-trained multilingual language models (BERT, RoBERTa, GPT-2) for Spanish."]}

methods: Naturalistic causal probing (input-level intervention on naturalistic sentences to create counterfactuals)
Estimation of average treatment effect (ATE) of morpho-syntactic features on contextualized representations
Causal analysis of gender bias using naturalistic counterfactuals
Comparison of correlational probes with causal probes using augmented datasets
problems: Limitations of traditional probing methods in identifying causal relationships in neural language model representations
Bias and lack of diversity in template-based counterfactual interventions
Overestimation of linguistic property encoding by correlational probes
Understanding how morpho-syntactic features (gender, number) are encoded and causally affect model representations
datasets: Spanish corpora (with naturalistic sentences and their counterfactuals)
metrics: Average Treatment Effect (ATE)
Probe accuracy (for gender and number prediction)

results.metric: Probe accuracy (correlational, augmented with naturalistic counterfactuals)
results.value: ~60% (close to chance)
Stable across corpora

novelty_claims: Proposes a naturalistic input-level intervention strategy to generate counterfactuals for causal probing, improving over template-based and representation-level interventions.
Demonstrates that naturalistic counterfactuals provide unbiased estimates of causal effects of morpho-syntactic features.
Shows that correlational probes overestimate the presence of linguistic properties, and that causal probing with naturalistic counterfactuals gives more accurate estimates.
Applies the method to analyze gender and number encoding and gender bias in pre-trained multilingual language models (BERT, RoBERTa, GPT-2) for Spanish.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["BEAR probe: a unified knowledge probing framework for both causal and masked language models using log-likelihood ranking of multiple-choice textual statements","Construction of a new evaluation dataset (BEAR) with carefully designed relational facts and answer options","Open-source evaluation framework (lm-pub-quiz) for applying the BEAR probe"],"problems":["Inability to compare relational knowledge across masked and causal language models due to differences in pre-training objectives","Limitations of existing probes (e.g., LAMA) such as restriction to single-token answers, exclusion of causal LMs, skewed answer spaces, and issues with multiple correct answers","Need for a more accurate and unified evaluation of relational knowledge in language models"],"datasets":["BEAR dataset (7,731 instances, 40,916 in larger variant)"],"metrics":["Accuracy (whether the LM assigns highest log-likelihood to the correct statement)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["BEAR is the first unified probing framework that can evaluate both masked and causal language models for relational knowledge using a log-likelihood ranking approach.","BEAR removes restrictions on answer space, allowing for multi-token and rare answers.","The BEAR dataset is carefully constructed to address known issues in prior datasets, such as answer skew and multiple correct answers.","The open-source BEAR framework and dataset enable standardized, fair comparison of a wide range of language models."]}

methods: BEAR probe: a unified knowledge probing framework for both causal and masked language models using log-likelihood ranking of multiple-choice textual statements
Construction of a new evaluation dataset (BEAR) with carefully designed relational facts and answer options
Open-source evaluation framework (lm-pub-quiz) for applying the BEAR probe
problems: Inability to compare relational knowledge across masked and causal language models due to differences in pre-training objectives
Limitations of existing probes (e.g., LAMA) such as restriction to single-token answers, exclusion of causal LMs, skewed answer spaces, and issues with multiple correct answers
Need for a more accurate and unified evaluation of relational knowledge in language models
datasets: BEAR dataset (7,731 instances, 40,916 in larger variant)
metrics: Accuracy (whether the LM assigns highest log-likelihood to the correct statement)
novelty_claims: BEAR is the first unified probing framework that can evaluate both masked and causal language models for relational knowledge using a log-likelihood ranking approach.
BEAR removes restrictions on answer space, allowing for multi-token and rare answers.
The BEAR dataset is carefully constructed to address known issues in prior datasets, such as answer skew and multiple correct answers.
The open-source BEAR framework and dataset enable standardized, fair comparison of a wide range of language models.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Usage-based probing setup combining behavioral, diagnostic, and causal probing paradigms","Causal intervention on model representations to remove specific linguistic property encodings","Analysis of information flow across BERT layers"],"problems":["Determining whether pre-trained language models (specifically BERT) actually use encoded linguistic properties (grammatical number) for predictions","Identifying the functional encoding of grammatical number in BERT and how it is used in number agreement tasks","Understanding how grammatical number information is transferred within BERT's layers"],"datasets":["Number agreement task dataset (likely based on subject–verb agreement in English; specific dataset name not provided)"],"metrics":["Task accuracy (on number agreement task)","Impact on task performance after intervention"],"results":[{"metric":"Task accuracy after removing functional encoding","value":"Significantly harmed, indicating BERT relies on the encoding"},{"metric":"Layer range for number information transfer","value":"Between 3rd and 8th BERT layers"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a usage-based probing setup to identify encodings actually used by the model, not just present in representations","Demonstration that BERT relies on a linear functional encoding of grammatical number for number agreement tasks","Discovery that BERT uses separate encodings for grammatical number in nouns and verbs","Identification of the specific BERT layers (3rd to 8th) where grammatical number information is transferred from noun to verb"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Introduction of a new suite of psycholinguistic diagnostic tests for language models, drawn from human language experiments","Application of these diagnostics to the BERT model to assess its linguistic capacities"],"problems":["Understanding what linguistic capacities are conferred to language models by pre-training","Identifying the strengths and weaknesses of language models like BERT in specific linguistic phenomena (e.g., inference, event prediction, negation)","Lack of targeted, controlled diagnostics for evaluating language models beyond syntactic abilities"],"datasets":["A new suite of psycholinguistic diagnostic datasets derived from human language experiments (specific datasets not named in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Word prediction accuracy","Sensitivity to distinctions between good and bad context completions"],"results":[{"metric":"Word prediction accuracy (category/role reversal)","value":"BERT can generally distinguish good from bad completions, but with less sensitivity than humans"},{"metric":"Noun-hypernym association","value":"BERT robustly retrieves noun hypernyms"},{"metric":"Commonsense/pragmatic inference and role-based event prediction","value":"BERT struggles with these tasks"},{"metric":"Sensitivity to negation","value":"BERT shows clear insensitivity and fails to generalize understanding of negation"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a new set of psycholinguistic diagnostic tests for language models, targeting a broader range of linguistic capacities than previous diagnostics","Diagnostics are carefully controlled and drawn from human experiments, allowing targeted evaluation of language models without task-specific fine-tuning","Diagnostics reveal insensitivities in predictive models and target capacities beyond syntax, including commonsense/pragmatic inference, semantic roles, event knowledge, category membership, and negation"]}

results.value: BERT can generally distinguish good from bad completions, but with less sensitivity than humans

results.metric: Commonsense/pragmatic inference and role-based event prediction
results.value: BERT struggles with these tasks
BERT shows clear insensitivity and fails to generalize understanding of negation

novelty_claims: Introduction of a new set of psycholinguistic diagnostic tests for language models, targeting a broader range of linguistic capacities than previous diagnostics
Diagnostics are carefully controlled and drawn from human experiments, allowing targeted evaluation of language models without task-specific fine-tuning

methods: Intrinsic information-theoretic framework for identifying concept subspaces in language model representations
Counterfactual framework for measuring mutual information between subspaces and concepts

Causal controlled generation via manipulation of concept subspace

novelty_claims: Proposes an intrinsic, information-theoretic framework for identifying and evaluating concept subspaces in language model representations, without reliance on auxiliary classifiers
Introduces a counterfactual approach to break dependence between concept subspace and its orthogonal complement, enabling accurate measurement of concept information
Defines four geometric properties (erasure, encapsulation, stability, containment) to precisely characterize concept encoding in representation space
Demonstrates causal controlled generation by manipulating concept subspaces, and empirically validates this for verbal-number but not grammatical-gender
Shows that do-intervention improves causal effect estimation over previous methods on the CEBaB benchmark

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Interchange Intervention Training (IIT): aligns variables in a causal model with neural network representations and trains the neural model to match the counterfactual behavior of the causal model via aligned interventions; fully differentiable and can be combined with other objectives"],"problems":["How to incorporate well-founded causal structure into neural networks while allowing data-driven learning","How to train neural networks to realize interpretable, abstract causal structures rather than just passively analyzing them","Improving systematic generalization and interpretability of neural models"],"datasets":["MNIST-PVR (structural vision task)","ReaSCAN (navigational language task)","MQNLI (natural language inference task)"],"metrics":["Systematic generalization accuracy (behavioral evaluation)","Structural evaluation (degree to which the neural model realizes the target causal model)"],"results":[{"metric":"Systematic generalization accuracy (across all tasks)","value":"IIT achieves the best results compared to multi-task training and data augmentation"},{"metric":"Structural evaluation (interpretability)","value":"IIT-trained models more successfully realize the target causal model than baselines"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Interchange Intervention Training (IIT), a new method for training neural networks to realize the abstract structure of a causal model, not just analyzing them post hoc","IIT is fully differentiable and guarantees that the target causal model is a causal abstraction of the neural model when loss is zero","IIT enables productive interplay between model analysis and model improvement by pushing models to acquire interpretable causal structure during training, rather than only evaluating it after training","Demonstrates that IIT leads to both better systematic generalization and more interpretable neural models compared to existing methods"]}

methods: Interchange Intervention Training (IIT): aligns variables in a causal model with neural network representations and trains the neural model to match the counterfactual behavior of the causal model via aligned interventions; fully differentiable and can be combined with other objectives
problems: How to incorporate well-founded causal structure into neural networks while allowing data-driven learning
How to train neural networks to realize interpretable, abstract causal structures rather than just passively analyzing them
Improving systematic generalization and interpretability of neural models
datasets: MNIST-PVR (structural vision task)
ReaSCAN (navigational language task)
MQNLI (natural language inference task)
metrics: Systematic generalization accuracy (behavioral evaluation)
Structural evaluation (degree to which the neural model realizes the target causal model)

results.value: IIT achieves the best results compared to multi-task training and data augmentation
IIT-trained models more successfully realize the target causal model than baselines

novelty_claims: Proposes Interchange Intervention Training (IIT), a new method for training neural networks to realize the abstract structure of a causal model, not just analyzing them post hoc
IIT is fully differentiable and guarantees that the target causal model is a causal abstraction of the neural model when loss is zero

IIT enables productive interplay between model analysis and model improvement by pushing models to acquire interpretable causal structure during training, rather than only evaluating it after training
Demonstrates that IIT leads to both better systematic generalization and more interpretable neural models compared to existing methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Iterative Null-space Projection (INLP): an iterative method that trains linear classifiers to predict a property to be removed, then projects representations onto the nullspace of the classifier, repeating this process to remove information about the property from the representation","Integration of INLP into pre-trained classifiers for fair classification"],"problems":["Removing specific kinds of information (e.g., protected attributes like gender, race, age) from neural representations such as word embeddings","Mitigating bias and increasing fairness in language-based classification tasks","Ensuring that classifiers' decisions are oblivious to protected attributes"],"datasets":["Word embeddings (e.g., GloVe vectors) for gender bias removal (as in Bolukbasi et al., 2016; methods: Zero-shot robustness evaluation of ChatGPT and other foundation models on adversarial and out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks
Comparison with several popular foundation models from Huggingface and OpenAI
Use of AdvGLUE and ANLI benchmarks for adversarial robustness
Use of Flipkart review and DDXPlus medical diagnosis datasets for OOD robustness
Creation of an adversarial translation dataset by sampling from AdvGLUE
problems: Evaluating the robustness of ChatGPT to adversarial and out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs
Assessing the performance of ChatGPT on unexpected or perturbed inputs, especially for safety-critical applications
Comparing ChatGPT's robustness to other foundation models
datasets: AdvGLUE
ANLI
Flipkart review
DDXPlus medical diagnosis
Adversarial translation dataset (30 samples from AdvGLUE)
metrics: Classification accuracy
Translation quality (readability and reasonableness of responses)

results.metric: Adversarial and OOD classification tasks (relative performance)
results.value: ChatGPT shows consistent improvements over most counterparts, but absolute performance is far from perfection
ChatGPT generates readable and reasonable responses, but is outperformed by text-davinci-003
ChatGPT outperforms other foundation models

novelty_claims: This is the first thorough evaluation of ChatGPT's robustness from both adversarial and out-of-distribution perspectives.
The study provides a comprehensive zero-shot robustness evaluation across multiple datasets and tasks.
The work highlights ChatGPT's strengths and weaknesses in adversarial and OOD settings, which had not been systematically studied before.

methods: Analyzing correlation structure of model performance across prompts in benchmarks
Weighting prompts during evaluation based on their correlation structure rather than treating all prompts equally
Characterizing model performance over the distribution of all possible prompt weights as a robustness check
Explaining performance similarity using semantic similarity and common LLM failure points
problems: The assumption that benchmark prompts are a random sample from a real-world distribution is often invalid
Model comparisons are brittle with respect to benchmark composition due to non-random correlations between prompts
Standard evaluation methods may not reflect true model performance across different use cases
Potential biases in benchmarks due to prompt similarity and distributional assumptions
datasets: Four major LLM evaluation benchmarks (specific names not provided in the excerpt)
metrics: Model performance (aggregate metric, e.g., accuracy or similar, not specified)
Correlation of model performance across prompts
Rank change of models

results.metric: Significance of correlation in model performance across prompts
results.value: p-value < 0.05 for each considered benchmark

results.metric: Shift in model performance due to different distributional assumptions
results.value: as large as 10%

results.metric: Rank change in model rankings due to different distributional assumptions
results.value: as large as 5 (out of 14 models)

novelty_claims: First to systematically analyze the impact of distributional assumptions and prompt correlations on LLM benchmark evaluation and model rankings
Propose a robustness check for model comparisons by characterizing performance over all possible prompt weights
Show that semantic similarity and common LLM failure points explain performance correlations across prompts

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["CONtinous pAtterNs (CONAN): a method where patterns are represented as continuous vectors (not restricted to discrete tokens from the PLM vocabulary) and optimized for lexical inference in context (LIiC) tasks"],"problems":["Improving lexical inference in context (LIiC), also known as predicate entailment, which is challenging for pretrained language models (PLMs) not specifically trained with lexical knowledge","Overcoming the limitations of discrete pattern-based approaches for PLMs, especially in low-resource settings"],"datasets":["Two established LIiC (lexical inference in context) benchmarks (exact names not specified in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Accuracy (implied by 'performance' and 'state of the art')"],"results":[{"metric":"State of the art performance on two LIiC benchmarks","value":"CONAN outperforms previous discrete pattern-based and baseline methods (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of CONAN, which allows patterns to be any continuous vector, not just discrete tokens from the PLM vocabulary","CONAN achieves new state of the art results on LIiC benchmarks without handcrafting or automatically mining patterns from corpora","CONAN is more efficient, using shorter patterns (as few as 2 tokens per pattern) compared to previous methods","Provides new insights into how patterns interact with PLMs and raises questions about PLM understanding of text patterns"]}

methods: CONtinous pAtterNs (CONAN): a method where patterns are represented as continuous vectors (not restricted to discrete tokens from the PLM vocabulary) and optimized for lexical inference in context (LIiC) tasks
problems: Improving lexical inference in context (LIiC), also known as predicate entailment, which is challenging for pretrained language models (PLMs) not specifically trained with lexical knowledge
Overcoming the limitations of discrete pattern-based approaches for PLMs, especially in low-resource settings
datasets: Two established LIiC (lexical inference in context) benchmarks (exact names not specified in the provided text)
metrics: Accuracy (implied by 'performance' and 'state of the art')

results.metric: State of the art performance on two LIiC benchmarks
results.value: CONAN outperforms previous discrete pattern-based and baseline methods (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: Introduction of CONAN, which allows patterns to be any continuous vector, not just discrete tokens from the PLM vocabulary
CONAN achieves new state of the art results on LIiC benchmarks without handcrafting or automatically mining patterns from corpora
CONAN is more efficient, using shorter patterns (as few as 2 tokens per pattern) compared to previous methods
Provides new insights into how patterns interact with PLMs and raises questions about PLM understanding of text patterns
